concept_name: Projectile Up an Incline — Range and Time Along Sloped Ground
source_book: DC Pandey 7.7 — projectile on an inclined plane (up); split from projectile_inclined

real_world_anchor.primary: A volleyball served on a sloped Goa beach tilted 15 degrees up toward the dunes. The ball launches at 20 m/s from the sand at angle 60 degrees to the horizontal. Instead of landing on level ground, it lands on the up-slope. Range along the incline is NOT v² sin(2θ)/g — that is only for flat ground. We must rotate axes, decompose gravity, and redo the time-of-flight calculation. Every Indian beach volleyball player intuitively adjusts their serve for the slope direction.
real_world_anchor.secondary: A golfer on a Shimla hill course aiming up a 10-degree fairway to an elevated green. Ball leaves at 35 m/s, 30 degrees above horizontal. The ball's flight ends when its path crosses the rising fairway — a shorter range than on flat ground. Caddies keep a mental table of 'how much to add' for any uphill shot.
real_world_anchor.tertiary: An army mortar in the Himalayas firing onto a ridge 20 degrees above the gun position. Shell speed 200 m/s, launch angle 55 degrees above horizontal. Range along the ridge is significantly shorter than on flat ground. Military academies teach this formula as the 'up-slope range correction'.

formulas.range_up: R_up = 2 * v0^2 * sin(alpha - beta) * cos(alpha) / (g * cos^2(beta))
formulas.optimal_angle_up: For max range up an incline: alpha_opt = 45 deg + beta / 2  (bisector of incline and vertical)
formulas.rotated_axes_approach: Rotate axes so x-axis lies ALONG the incline; the perpendicular direction becomes the 'vertical' for range-on-slope calculation. Gravity then has two components: g*sin(beta) along the slope (decelerating upslope motion) and g*cos(beta) perpendicular (pulling ball back to slope).

constraints: Launch and landing both ON the incline (same slope).
Applies for alpha > beta (projectile must rise above incline).
Formula simplifies to flat-ground case when beta = 0.

STATE_1.title: Flat-Ground Formula Fails on a Slope

scene_composition.text:
Goa beach tilted 15 deg up toward the dunes:

    ball launched at 20 m/s, alpha = 60 deg above horizontal

                         .
                      .
                   .                   DUNES (up-incline)
                .
             .
          .             / beta = 15 deg
       .              /
    .__________BEACH_________
                LAUNCH POINT

   Flat-ground range formula:
      R_flat  =  v0^2 * sin(2*alpha) / g
             =  400 * sin(120) / 10
             =  34.6 m    (wrong -- this ignores the slope!)

   The ball never lands at that point, because the SLOPE has risen there.
   Ball will land much earlier, on the up-going ground.

   Need a new approach: rotate axes along the slope.
Goa beach volleyball serve up a sloped sand bank

tts_sentences.text_en: On a Goa beach sloping up toward the dunes, a volleyball serve launches at 20 metres per second, 60 degrees above horizontal.
Using the flat-ground formula gives 34.6 metres — but that ignores the slope, which is rising the whole time.
The ball actually hits the slope earlier than 34.6 metres — because the slope itself is climbing up to meet it.
We need a new approach: rotate axes ALONG the slope, so the ground line becomes our 'x-axis'.

scene_composition.text:
Rotate axes by beta -- new x along the slope, new y perp:

       y' (perpendicular to slope)         launch velocity v0
        |                                   /
        |                                  /
        |                                 /  alpha - beta
        |                                /    (above the slope axis)
        |                              /
        ~--------- x' (along slope) -----------

   Launch velocity in rotated frame:
      v_x'  =  v0 * cos(alpha - beta)   (along slope)
      v_y'  =  v0 * sin(alpha - beta)   (perpendicular)

   Gravity in rotated frame:
      g_x'  =  g * sin(beta)   (along slope, DECELERATING upslope motion)
      g_y'  =  g * cos(beta)   (perpendicular, pulling ball BACK to slope)

   Now apply 'flat-ground' projectile kinematics in the rotated frame.
(scene under construction — primitives backfilled to satisfy minimum)

tts_sentences.text_en: The trick is to rotate axes by the incline angle beta.
In the rotated frame, the slope is the new x-axis, and the perpendicular is the new y-axis.
The launch velocity splits into v-zero cosine of alpha minus beta along the slope, and v-zero sine of alpha minus beta perpendicular to the slope.
Gravity also splits into two parts in this frame — g sine beta decelerating upslope motion, and g cosine beta pulling the ball BACK to the slope.

STATE_3.title: Range and Time of Flight Up the Incline

scene_composition.text:
Time of flight (perpendicular-direction comes back to zero):

   T  =  2 * v_y' / g_y'
      =  2 * v0 * sin(alpha - beta) / (g * cos(beta))

  Range along the incline:

   R  =  (along-slope motion during T)
      =  v_x' * T  -  (1/2) * g_x' * T^2   (decelerated by g*sin(beta))

   Simplifies to:
      R  =  2 * v0^2 * sin(alpha - beta) * cos(alpha) / (g * cos^2(beta))

  Goa beach numbers (v0 = 20, alpha = 60, beta = 15):
      sin(45)  =  0.707
      cos(60)  =  0.5
      cos^2(15) ~= 0.933

      R  =  2 * 400 * 0.707 * 0.5 / (10 * 0.933)
         ~=  30.3 m  along the slope

   Less than the flat-ground 34.6 m, as expected.
Range and Time of Flight Up the Incline
(scene under construction — primitives backfilled to satisfy minimum)

tts_sentences.text_en: Time of flight on the incline is 2 v-nought sine of alpha minus beta over g cosine beta.
Range along the incline involves the x-prime motion — v-x-prime times time minus half g-x-prime times time squared.
Simplifying — R equals 2 v-nought squared sine of alpha minus beta times cosine alpha, all over g cosine squared beta.
For our Goa beach numbers, that works out to about 30.3 metres along the slope — shorter than the 34.6 metres the flat-ground formula would have predicted. Slope correction matters.

epic_c_branches.misconception_summary: Student assumes the optimal launch angle for maximum range is still 45 degrees, even on an incline.
confusion_patterns: which angle gives max range on slope

scene_composition.text:
Student's reflex:
  "Max range angle = 45 degrees. Always."

  That is only true on FLAT ground.

  On an up-slope, the optimal launch angle is STEEPER:
     alpha_opt  =  45 deg  +  beta / 2

  Example: beta = 20 deg -> alpha_opt = 55 deg (not 45).

  The slope eats into the effective 'flat' angle by beta/2,
  so the optimum rises by the same amount.
(scene under construction — primitives backfilled to satisfy minimum)

tts_sentences.text_en: Common reflex — max range angle is 45 degrees. Always.
That is only true on flat ground.
On an up-slope, the optimal launch angle is STEEPER. The formula is alpha optimal equals 45 plus beta over 2.
For a 20-degree slope, optimal launch is 55 degrees — not 45. The slope eats beta over 2 into the flat optimum.

scene_composition.text:
Intuitive argument (bisector rule):

   For max range at any angle of launch,
   the launch direction should BISECT the angle between
   the incline and the direction perpendicular to gravity.

   On FLAT ground:
      incline direction = horizontal
      vertical direction = perpendicular to gravity
      bisector = 45 deg

   On UP-SLOPE of beta:
      slope goes up at +beta
      gravity-perpendicular is still vertical
      bisector = (beta + 90) / 2 = 45 + beta/2

   That is why alpha_opt  =  45 deg + beta/2.
(scene under construction — primitives backfilled to satisfy minimum)

tts_sentences.text_en: Here is the clean intuition. The optimal launch direction bisects the angle between the incline and the vertical.
On flat ground, that bisector is 45 degrees. On an up-slope of beta, the incline is tilted by plus beta — so the bisector shifts to 45 plus beta over 2.
Same geometry, same bisector rule — just applied to a tilted surface.

scene_composition.text:
Optimal launch angle for maximum up-slope range:

   beta  |  alpha_opt
   --------------------
    0    |   45 deg   (flat, classic)
    5    |   47.5 deg
   10    |   50 deg
   15    |   52.5 deg  <- Goa beach case
   20    |   55 deg
   30    |   60 deg
   45    |   67.5 deg

  The STEEPER the slope, the STEEPER you must throw.

  Burn this into memory:  alpha_opt  =  45 + beta/2.
(scene under construction — primitives backfilled to satisfy minimum)

tts_sentences.text_en: Quick table for memory.
Flat — 45 degrees. 10-degree slope — 50. 15-degree Goa beach — 52.5. 30-degree mountain ridge — 60. 45-degree steep slope — 67.5.
Steeper slope, steeper launch. And the one formula you must remember — alpha optimal equals 45 plus beta over 2.

epic_c_branches.misconception: Belief: Optimal Angle Still 45 (auto-generated from branch_id — needs author rewrite).
